1.synthesis: Chapter 11 opens with a formal commissioning: the word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH (Jeremiah 11:1). What follows is one of the most developed covenant-lawsuit forms in the prophetic corpus. The word berit (covenant) appears seven times in verses 1–11, structuring the passage as a sustained covenant-proclamation rather than a spontaneous oracle. Jeremiah is not composing a new message—he is reading out an indictment based on the stipulations of Mosaic covenant law, grounded specifically in the curses of Deuteronomy 27:26: Cursed is the man who does not hear the words of this covenant (Jeremiah 11:3). The covenant God made with Israel at the Exodus was sealed with the formula I will be your God and you shall be my people (Jeremiah 11:4)—the most compact expression of the bilateral covenant relationship. But Israel has repeatedly broken the covenant, each generation repeating the pattern of their ancestors (Jeremiah 11:7–8). The result is the covenant-curse mechanism activated: YHWH will bring upon them all the words of this covenant, which I commanded them to do, but they did not (Jeremiah 11:8). The conspiracy of Jeremiah 11:9–10 is the specific charge: Judah and the house of Israel have conspired together to return to the sins of their ancestors, going after other gods. The plural as many gods as you have cities, O Judah (Jeremiah 11:13) captures the scandal of polytheistic proliferation: every city has its local Baal altars. The famous olive-tree image of Jeremiah 11:16 appears later in the chapter: YHWH once called Israel a green olive tree, beautiful with good fruit—but now the tree is set ablaze. The olive tree is one of the most valuable and long-lived of Palestinian cultivars, associated with prosperity, peace, and permanence. Its burning is the covenant curse realized in agricultural terms. YHWH's planting of the tree (who planted you) and now his decreeing of disaster against it closes the covenant-theology circuit: the same Creator who gave the gift now removes it.
1.historical_context: The chapter belongs to the early phase of Jeremiah's ministry, likely during or after Josiah's reform (621 BCE) when the “book of the covenant” was found in the Temple (2 Kings 22:8–13). Jeremiah's commission to “proclaim in the cities of Judah” (Jeremiah 11:6) suggests active itinerant preaching. The berit language and Deuteronomic curse-formula (v. 3) reflect the theological framework of Deuteronomy 27–28 that shaped Josiah's reform. The mention of both Judah and Israel conspiring together (v. 10) may refer to the northern and southern kingdoms' shared apostasy, underscoring that the coming judgment does not merely punish a recent generation but settles the accumulated covenant debt of both kingdoms.
1.literary_structure: The lawsuit divides into YHWH's commissioning of Jeremiah (vv. 1–5), the covenant-history rehearsal with its charge of rebellion (vv. 6–10), the judgment decree of polytheistic city-Baals (vv. 11–13), and the olive-tree lament and disaster-decree (vv. 14–17). The sevenfold berit in vv. 1–11 creates a structural spine. The transition from legal to metaphorical (olive-tree) at v. 16 intensifies the emotional register of the judgment.
1.theological_application: The covenant-lawsuit form reveals that YHWH's judgment is not arbitrary but legally grounded in prior covenant stipulations Israel agreed to. The indictment does not move from emotion to punishment but from agreed-upon law to consequence. This models a theology of accountability: Israel's suffering is not YHWH's capricious anger but the activation of the covenant-curse mechanism that Israel itself ratified. The olive-tree image (Jeremiah 11:16) also carries a pastoral implication: Israel was genuinely beautiful and fruitful—YHWH is not destroying something that was never good but lamenting the destruction of something he created and cherished.
1.christology: Paul deploys the olive-tree image of Jeremiah 11:16 in Romans 11:17–24, describing Gentile believers as wild branches grafted into the cultivated olive tree of Israel. The covenant-lawsuit form anticipates the New Covenant (Jeremiah 31:31–34), where the problem of covenant-breaking is addressed not by renewed stipulations but by internalized law. Christ is the covenant keeper in whom the olive tree is restored and the Gentiles incorporated.

14.pericope_label: Do Not Pray for This People
14.synthesis: This brief unit contains two of the most theologically confronting elements in the book of Jeremiah: the prohibition of intercession and the lament over the olive tree. YHWH's command in Jeremiah 11:14—do not pray for this people, or lift up a cry or prayer on their behalf, for I will not listen when they call to me in the time of their trouble—is a direct reversal of the prophetic office. Prophets were intercessors; Moses and Samuel stood before YHWH on Israel's behalf (cf. Jeremiah 15:1). Now YHWH closes the court to intercession entirely. The three-fold prohibition (do not pray… do not lift up… I will not listen) creates the theological crisis: if prayer is unavailable and YHWH's ears are closed, the covenant's mercy-channel is sealed. The marriage imagery of Jeremiah 11:15—what right has my beloved in my house, when she has done many vile deeds?—surfaces the covenantal intimacy that has been violated. Israel is YHWH's yedidah (beloved), but she has profaned the covenant relationship so completely that her continued presence in the Temple is itself a violation. Even the sacred flesh of sacrifices cannot atone (Jeremiah 11:15)—ritual without covenant fidelity is empty. The olive tree of Jeremiah 11:16—beautiful, green, fruitful—is now being set ablaze by YHWH himself, despite its beauty, because of the evil Israel has done. The final verse (Jeremiah 11:17) makes the agency explicit: YHWH who planted the tree has decreed the disaster against it. The covenant Planter becomes the Destroyer when covenant violations accumulate beyond the threshold of mercy.
14.historical_context: The intercession-prohibition of Jeremiah 11:14 appears three times in the book (Jeremiah 7:16; Jeremiah 11:14; Jeremiah 14:11), marking escalating stages of covenant closure. Historically, the olive tree was an irreplaceable agricultural resource in ancient Palestine; burning olive trees was a particularly devastating form of warfare precisely because olive trees take decades to mature. The image captures the irreversibility of the judgment Jeremiah announces.
14.literary_structure: The unit is compact (four verses) but dense. Verse 14 is the intercession-prohibition. Verse 15 is a rhetorical question using marriage imagery. Verse 16 is the olive-tree lament. Verse 17 closes with the naming of YHWH's agency. The movement from legal (v. 14) to relational (v. 15) to horticultural (v. 16) to declaratory (v. 17) illustrates how Jeremiah layers covenant, marriage, and creation metaphors within a single oracle unit.
14.theological_application: The closing of intercession does not mean YHWH is permanently inaccessible but that a specific moment of covenant judgment is sealed. The prophetic role of standing in the gap is vital (cf. Ezekiel 22:30), which is precisely why its prohibition is so catastrophic. The passage challenges any casual theology of continuous unconditional access to divine mercy: there are moments when the accumulated weight of covenant violation places a community beyond the threshold where intercession can alter the trajectory. Yet even here, YHWH's grief is visible in his having planted and named this tree as beautiful.
14.christology: The intercession-prohibition finds its ultimate resolution in Christ as the eternal intercessor who always lives to make intercession for his people (Hebrews 7:25). Where Jeremiah is forbidden to intercede because the old covenant has run its course, Jesus intercedes on the basis of a new covenant secured by his blood (Hebrews 12:24). The beloved-in-the-house imagery anticipates the Johannine language of abiding in Christ—the only place where the covenant beloved legitimately dwells (John 15:4–5).

18.synthesis: The chapter closes with the first of Jeremiah's Confessions—biographical material in which the prophet describes his discovery of a plot against his life. The narrative shift of Jeremiah 11:18—YHWH made it known to me and I knew; then you showed me their deeds—is jarring: from covenant-lawsuit against Israel to YHWH warning his own prophet of a conspiracy by his own people. The disclosure formula (made it known to me) signals divine protection of the prophet even as the community seeks to silence him. Jeremiah's self-description in Jeremiah 11:19 is one of the most poignant in the prophetic literature: I was like a gentle lamb led to the slaughter. I did not know it was against me they devised schemes, saying, Let us destroy the tree with its fruit; let us cut him off from the land of the living, that his name be remembered no more. The lamb imagery captures innocence and ignorance. Jeremiah did not know he was being plotted against; he went about his prophetic mission unaware of the conspiracy being planned by the men of Anathoth, his hometown. The conspirators' language is revealing: they want to destroy the tree with its fruit, to cut him off from the land of the living and erase his name. These are the most comprehensive forms of elimination in the ANE—physical death plus memorial death. Jeremiah's response is not vengeance but a covenant appeal: O YHWH of hosts, who judges righteously, who tests the heart and the mind, let me see your vengeance upon them, for to you have I committed my cause (Jeremiah 11:20). The men of Anathoth have demanded he stop prophesying under threat of death (Jeremiah 11:21). YHWH's response is precise judgment: the men who seek Jeremiah's life will be punished—their young men by the sword, their sons and daughters by famine (Jeremiah 11:22–23). The judgment mirrors the covenant-curse categories of the sword-and-famine that Jeremiah himself was proclaiming against Judah as a whole.
18.historical_context: Anathoth was Jeremiah's hometown in the territory of Benjamin (Jeremiah 1:1), a priestly village. The plot against him by his own townspeople and family reflects the social cost of prophetic vocation: Jeremiah's countercultural message alienated him from his own community first. The lamb-to-slaughter image of v. 19 is embedded in Second Temple interpretation of the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53:7—a lamb silent before its shearers—making Jeremiah's Confession a major intertextual node in the prophetic portrait of righteous suffering.
18.literary_structure: The Confession divides into YHWH's disclosure (v. 18), Jeremiah's self-description and the conspirators' plan (v. 19), the covenant appeal (v. 20), the specific threat from Anathoth (v. 21), and YHWH's judgment oracle (vv. 22–23). The lamb-image of v. 19 links back to the olive-tree of v. 16: Jeremiah has identified himself with the very thing the community is destroying.
18.theological_application: The first Confession establishes the pattern of Jeremiah's spiritual life: disclosure of threat → honest complaint to YHWH → covenant appeal → divine vindication. The prophet does not seek personal revenge but commits his cause to the divine judge who tests heart and mind (Jeremiah 11:20). The lamb imagery establishes innocence as the ground of appeal: Jeremiah's vulnerability is not weakness but the posture of one who has no recourse but YHWH. This is the prophetic vocation in its most exposed form.
18.christology: The lamb-led-to-slaughter image of Jeremiah 11:19, shared with Isaiah 53:7, is one of the most direct prophetic prefigurations of the passion of Christ. Jesus, like Jeremiah, was plotted against by his own people and homeland. The appeal to YHWH as who tests the heart and mind (v. 20) finds its New Testament echo in the risen Christ described as the one who searches minds and hearts (Revelation 2:23). Jeremiah's commitment of his cause to the divine judge anticipates Christ's trust in him who judges justly (1 Peter 2:23).
